network.overall_strategy: This network is literature-guided and explicitly distinguishes strong support for prebiotic acetyl thioester chemistry from the still unresolved synthesis of full acetyl-CoA. The core of the network follows demonstrated routes to carbon monoxide, formate, acetate, pyruvate, methanethiol, S-methyl thioacetate, acetyl phosphate, pantetheine, and acetyl-pantetheine-like thioesters across hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical settings. The final acetyl-CoA node is included as a speculative target reached through transfer of an acetyl group onto a CoA-like thiol framework after pantetheine emergence, because the supplied literature identifies this as an open problem rather than an established prebiotic synthesis. Ten distinct pathways converge through hub molecules CO, formate, pyruvate, methanethiol, and S-methyl thioacetate, with hydrothermal mineral catalysis feeding surface photochemistry and then biochemical thiol/thioester exchange chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate on greigite/magnetite/awaruite analog surfaces

conditions.pressure: alkaline hydrothermal, H2/CO2; reported at elevated pressure in sealed reactors
conditions.catalyst: greigite, magnetite; hydrothermal mineral systems including Ni3Fe/awaruite analogs
conditions.other: alkaline solution; vent-like conditions; formate up to 200 mM reported

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed hydrogenation/electroreduction of CO2 gives the first stable C1 product, formate.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite, magnetite, and awaruite catalyze H2 + CO2 conversion to formate, acetate, methanol, and pyruvate at 100C under alkaline hydrothermal conditions; this implements the first stable reduction step emphasized by Russell and Martin (2007).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to carbon monoxide on Ni-Fe sulfide surfaces

conditions.catalyst: Ni-bearing FeS/greigite-like sulfide surfaces under vent redox gradients
conditions.other: alkaline vent micropores; pH gradient about 3-5 units; geoelectrochemical driving force about 250-300 mV

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is electroreduced on Ni-Fe sulfide surfaces to surface-bound or dissolved CO, analogous to CODH chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Wachtershauser (1988) proposed CO generation on FeS/NiS surfaces as part of surface metabolism; Russell (1994) and Martin & Russell (2007) framed alkaline vent CO2-to-CO reduction as a Wood-Ljungdahl analog. Kitadai et al. (2021) directly observed CO accumulation on partially reduced NiS under geoelectrochemical conditions.

reactions.name: Native iron reduction of CO2 to acetate and pyruvate

conditions.other: anoxic; direct CO2 reduction by Fe0 without biological electron bifurcation

reactions.mechanism: Oxidation of Fe0 provides reducing equivalents that drive CO2 fixation to formate, acetate, and pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) showed Fe0 reduces CO2 to acetate, pyruvate, and formate at 30-100C under 1-40 bar CO2. This route is a major literature-supported convergent source of both acetate and pyruvate.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from H2 and CO2 on greigite/magnetite

conditions.other: alkaline conditions; pyruvate detected at low micromolar levels

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-catalyzed C1 reduction and C-C bond formation yields pyruvate as part of a nonenzymatic Wood-Ljungdahl/rTCA-like product network.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) reported pyruvate formation from H2 + CO2 on greigite, magnetite, and awaruite at 100C, directly supporting hydrothermal production of this hub metabolite.

reactions.name: High-pressure FeS-carbonyl pyruvate synthesis from CO and thiols

conditions.other: deep hydrothermal high-pressure system; alkyl thiols required

reactions.mechanism: CO reacts with FeS and alkyl thiols to generate organometallic Fe-S-carbonyl intermediates that yield pyruvic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Cody et al. (2000) demonstrated pyruvate synthesis from CO + FeS + alkyl thiols at 250C and 50-200 MPa, providing a distinct hydrothermal route to pyruvate through organometallic intermediates.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic surface synthesis of pyruvate precursor network

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfide minerals and photocatalytic mineral surfaces
conditions.other: HCN/H2S-driven cyanosulfidic chemistry under UV irradiation; precursor pooling step representing documented systems chemistry

reactions.mechanism: HCN-based photoredox chemistry builds activated nitrile intermediates that feed into pyruvate-forming surface networks.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) established cyanosulfidic protometabolism from HCN and H2S under UV photoredox conditions. This reaction is included as a connecting precursor step because the supplied literature identifies the Sutherland framework as the source of pyruvate-forming surface chemistry.

reactions.name: UV-driven surface formation of pyruvate from cyanosulfidic alpha-hydroxy acid network

conditions.other: cyanosulfidic photoredox regime; pyruvate reported in about 14% yield from alpha-hydroxy acid oxidation

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven oxidation within the cyanosulfidic network converts alpha-hydroxy acid precursors into pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson (2021) reported UV-driven formation of pyruvate in the Sutherland-style cyanosulfidic network at about 14% yield; Patel et al. (2015) provides the broader systems chemistry context. This is the surface branch to the pyruvate hub.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal NiS/FeS carbonylation of methanethiol to S-methyl thioacetate

conditions.catalyst: NiS/FeS coprecipitates on sulfide mineral surfaces
conditions.other: sealed anaerobic vials; initial pH about 8; 7 days for mixed sulfides, 20 h for NiS alone

reactions.mechanism: CO binds Ni centers, methyl-thiol activation generates Ni-CH3 equivalents, migratory insertion forms an acetyl intermediate, and thiolysis releases S-methyl thioacetate.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wachtershauser (1997) experimentally demonstrated methyl thioacetate formation from CO and methanethiol on NiS/FeS at 100C, establishing the core prebiotic acetyl thioester synthesis route.

reactions.name: Room-temperature geoelectrochemical synthesis of S-methyl thioacetate on partially reduced NiS

conditions.catalyst: partially reduced NiS (NiS_PERM), optionally with CoS
conditions.other: neutral pH; electrochemical potential from -0.6 to -1.0 V vs SHE; surface CO accumulation; up to 35% yield, 56% with CoS

reactions.mechanism: CO2 is electroreduced to surface-bound CO on partially reduced Ni sulfides, followed by carbonylation of methanethiol to S-methyl thioacetate.
reactions.reasoning: Kitadai et al. (2021) showed NiS_PERM formed under geoelectrochemical conditions catalyzes methyl thioacetate synthesis at room temperature and neutral pH with greatly improved yields. This is the strongest direct literature route to the thioester hub.

reactions.name: UV decarboxylative thioesterification of pyruvate with disulfide to thioacetate ester

conditions.other: anoxic surface pool; disulfide-based sulfur donor required; no external oxidant

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes UV-driven oxidative decarboxylation in the presence of disulfides to yield activated thioacetate esters.
reactions.reasoning: Leqraa et al. (2020) demonstrated that pyruvate reacts with disulfides under UV irradiation to form thioacetate esters without an external oxidant. The network uses dimethyl disulfide as the sulfur-state-correct precursor to S-methyl thioacetate, correcting earlier over-simplification.

reactions.name: Thioester-phosphate exchange from S-methyl thioacetate to acetyl phosphate

conditions.other: requires inorganic phosphate in neutral to mildly alkaline aqueous solution

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic phosphate attack on the thioester carbonyl transfers the acetyl group from sulfur to phosphate, generating acetyl phosphate.
reactions.reasoning: Martin & Russell (2007) emphasized acetyl phosphate as a plausible primordial energy currency formed from acetyl thioesters and inorganic phosphate. The supplied literature explicitly identifies acetyl phosphate as a bridge between thioester and phosphate metabolism.

reactions.name: Prebiotic synthesis of pantetheine from nitrile-based precursor chemistry

conditions.other: room-temperature aqueous chemoselective nitrile chemistry; multistep network condensed to preserve connectivity

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile-based aqueous chemistry constructs the pantetheine scaffold as the catalytically active CoA subunit.
reactions.reasoning: Fairchild et al. (2024) reported the first prebiotically plausible synthesis of pantetheine through chemoselective nitrile chemistry in water at room temperature. This reaction is intentionally compressed as a literature-grounded network summary, not as a claim of direct one-step conversion from HCN alone.

reactions.name: Thiol-thioester exchange between S-methyl thioacetate and pantetheine to form acetyl pantetheine

conditions.other: neutral pH favored; thiol-thioester exchange outcompetes hydrolysis on suitable timescales

reactions.mechanism: Pantetheine thiol attacks the thioester carbonyl of S-methyl thioacetate, transferring the acetyl group and releasing methanethiol.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature notes that thiol-thioester exchange is substantially faster than thioester hydrolysis and that pantetheine can replace CoA in proto-metabolic network models (Goldford et al., 2017; Brosnan et al., 2011 as summarized in the report). This step yields an acetyl-pantetheine proto-cofactor rather than incorrectly claiming pantetheine synthesis from simple thiols.

reactions.name: Speculative late-stage conversion of acetyl pantetheine and CoA framework to acetyl-CoA

conditions.other: thiol-thioester exchange in concentrated prebiotic pools or interfaces; speculative because full CoA prebiotic assembly remains unresolved

reactions.mechanism: Acetyl transfer by thiol-thioester exchange from acetyl pantetheine to a CoA-like thiol acceptor gives acetyl-CoA.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative and included only to connect the literature-supported acetyl-thioester network to the requested target molecule. The research report states that the CoA complexity gap remains unresolved, while Fairchild et al. (2024) narrows the gap via pantetheine synthesis. Therefore the network treats full acetyl-CoA formation as an open problem rather than an established prebiotic reaction.

pathways.name: Wachtershauser iron-sulfur carbonylation route
pathways.description: Direct hydrothermal thioester synthesis from CO2-derived CO and methanethiol on sulfide minerals.

pathways.name: Kitadai geoelectrochemical room-temperature route
pathways.description: CO2 electroreduction and methyl thioacetate synthesis on partially reduced NiS at neutral pH, then acetyl transfer to pantetheine and CoA-like acceptor.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate-first vent route
pathways.description: Alkaline vent reduction produces formate, then parallel vent CO production converges into methyl thioacetate formation.

pathways.name: Native iron acetate-pyruvate fixation route
pathways.description: Direct Fe0 reduction of CO2 produces pyruvate/formate/acetate, feeding downstream thioester chemistry through pyruvate and surface or hydrothermal convergence.

pathways.name: Preiner hydrothermal pyruvate branch
pathways.description: Greigite/magnetite generate pyruvate from H2 and CO2, followed by transfer to the surface UV thioesterification branch.

pathways.name: Cody high-pressure organometallic pyruvate branch
pathways.description: Deep hydrothermal FeS-carbonyl chemistry makes pyruvate, which later enters surface UV-mediated thioacetate formation and biochemical transfer.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic surface pyruvate route
pathways.description: HCN-based surface protometabolism yields pyruvate, which is photochemically converted to thioacetate ester and then to acetyl pantetheine.

pathways.name: Pantetheine-first proto-cofactor route
pathways.description: Surface pantetheine synthesis proceeds independently and later captures acetyl groups from hydrothermal methyl thioacetate.

pathways.name: Acetyl phosphate bridge route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal methyl thioacetate is converted to acetyl phosphate as an energy-rich intermediate before the acetyl group is considered available for later biochemical capture.

pathways.name: Dual-convergence vent-to-surface-to-biochemical route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO and formate production, surface pyruvate generation, and pantetheine synthesis converge on a shared thioester hub and then the speculative acetyl-CoA endpoint.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongly aligned with literature up to S-methyl thioacetate formation: CO generation on Ni-Fe sulfides and carbonylation of methanethiol are well supported by Wächtershäuser-type and Kitadai-style chemistry. Transfer from methyl thioacetate to pantetheine is plausible, but the final conversion to full acetyl-CoA remains explicitly speculative and is not demonstrated in the supplied literature. This pathway is therefore good for acetyl-thioester chemistry, but weak for the stated target.
suggestions: Reframe the endpoint as acetyl-pantetheine or S-methyl thioacetate unless a literature-supported route to CoA assembly is added.
Clarify whether pantetheine is intended as the true functional proto-cofactor instead of full CoA.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is one of the most literature-faithful subroutes for methyl thioacetate production, matching the room-temperature geoelectrochemical NiS_PERM scenario reported by Kitadai et al. However, the route still inherits the unresolved jump from acetyl-thioester chemistry to full acetyl-CoA. As written, it convincingly reaches a thioester hub, not the target molecule.
suggestions: Use this route as a benchmark pathway to acetyl thioesters rather than acetyl-CoA.
Add stronger justification or literature support for methanethiol availability and CoA-framework emergence.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a chemically coherent vent-centered route that matches the literature well: formate and CO from H2/CO2 on hydrothermal minerals, carbonylation to methyl thioacetate, and plausible conversion to acetyl phosphate and pantetheine thioesters. It succeeds as a proto-metabolic acetyl-thioester pathway. Confidence is reduced only because it does not independently resolve synthesis of full CoA or a demonstrated route to acetyl-CoA.
suggestions: Explicitly include acetyl phosphate as an alternative endpoint or energetic branch product.
Strengthen literature grounding for thioester exchange onto pantetheine under prebiotic conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The native iron reduction of CO2 to acetate, pyruvate, and formate is strongly literature-supported. The subsequent leap from pyruvate-derived chemistry to S-methyl thioacetate via UV/disulfide chemistry is plausible but less directly integrated with the iron route, and the final acetyl-CoA step remains unsupported. Overall, this pathway is chemically mixed and target-limited.
suggestions: Clarify the provenance of dimethyl disulfide and whether methyl thioacetate rather than generic thioacetate ester is specifically formed.
Keep this branch focused on pyruvate-to-thioester chemistry without claiming validated access to acetyl-CoA.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is well aligned with Preiner-style mineral-catalyzed pyruvate synthesis from H2 and CO2 and sensibly connects hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface photochemical thioesterification and later biochemical exchange chemistry. It is a strong vent-to-surface proto-metabolic pathway, but again reaches supported acetyl-thioester analog chemistry more convincingly than full acetyl-CoA.
suggestions: State more explicitly that the route validates pyruvate-centered convergence and activated acetyl transfer, not complete CoA biosynthesis.
Provide conditions for thioester-phosphate exchange and pantetheine trapping.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Cody-style high-pressure organometallic pyruvate synthesis is literature-supported and appropriately distinct from vent H2/CO2 reduction. However, conversion from pyruvate into the exact S-methyl thioacetate node via UV disulfide chemistry is less direct, and the pathway still ends in a speculative CoA-transfer step. Good as a convergent branch to pyruvate, but not as a validated target route.
suggestions: Use this pathway primarily to support pyruvate as a hub rather than as a complete route to acetyl-CoA.
Add a better-defined methyl-thiol source and clarify whether the pyruvate product is pyruvic acid or pyruvate under pathway conditions.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanosulfidic branch captures the spirit of Sutherland-type protometabolism and the literature connection from pyruvate to UV thioesterification. The weakest point is the simplification of complex nitrile network chemistry into HCN directly to malononitrile and then pyruvate; that abstraction is not clearly literature-faithful. This pathway is useful as a convergence branch, but not strongly demonstrated in its present compressed form.
suggestions: Expand the cyanosulfidic sequence with explicit alpha-hydroxy acid or nitrile intermediates described in the literature.
Specify the required mineral/photoredox agents for the surface steps more concretely.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pantetheine-first concept aligns with the major 2024 breakthrough and appropriately recognizes pantetheine as the functional subunit that narrows the CoA complexity gap. But the network compresses pantetheine synthesis into a single HCN-derived step, which understates the specific nitrile chemistry of Fairchild et al. The final CoA-related transformation remains unsupported, so the pathway is conceptually promising but incompletely grounded.
suggestions: Represent pantetheine synthesis with more realistic precursor nodes matching the reported nitrile chemistry.
Treat acetyl-pantetheine as the biologically meaningful endpoint if CoA assembly cannot be justified.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway is strong through the thioester-to-acetyl-phosphate bridge, which is very well aligned with Russell/Martin style protoenergetics and the literature emphasis on acetyl phosphate as a plausible primordial energy currency. Its weakness is the attempt to extend this well-supported bridge to full acetyl-CoA without a demonstrated route to the adenylated CoA scaffold.
suggestions: Elevate acetyl phosphate as a major validated endpoint.
Avoid implying that acetyl phosphate automatically solves the CoA assembly problem.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: As a whole-network route, this is the best representation of environmental interconnection: hydrothermal C1 fixation and thioester synthesis feeding surface photochemistry and then biochemical thiol/thioester exchange. It is topologically and conceptually strong, and its acknowledgement that full acetyl-CoA synthesis is unresolved is scientifically responsible. Nevertheless, because the network still formally claims acetyl-CoA production via a speculative final step, confidence remains below approval for the stated target.
suggestions: Redefine the target as a validated prebiotic acetyl thioester system or acetyl-pantetheine proto-cofactor network.
If acetyl-CoA must remain the target, explicitly lower confidence and provide a separate speculative module for CoA completion.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is topologically strong and literature-aware, with good environmental connectivity and multiple convergent routes to chemically central hubs such as CO, formate, pyruvate, methanethiol, and S-methyl thioacetate. It aligns well with the supplied literature on hydrothermal CO2 reduction, Ni/Fe-sulfide carbonylation, native iron fixation, Cody-style pyruvate formation, pyruvate-mediated thioesterification, acetyl phosphate bridging, and the importance of pantetheine as a proto-cofactor. The major limitation is target definition: the literature supports prebiotic acetyl thioesters and pantetheine far more strongly than full acetyl-CoA, and the network itself admits the final acetyl-CoA step is speculative. Additional issues include overcompression of the pantetheine synthesis chemistry, some ambiguity between pyruvic acid and pyruvate as separate nodes, and likely under-specification of surface mineral/photoredox agents for the cyanosulfidic branch. Overall, this is a good network for prebiotic acetyl-thioester chemistry, but not an approved network for bona fide acetyl-CoA synthesis.
